turns.text: Hello Candis, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I’m happy to help, though I’ll be honest—it’s a strange feeling being on this side of the research. Usually, I’m the one handing out the forms, not filling them out. 

Honestly, being forty-two and doing this solo is… it’s a lot. Every time I walk into my OB’s office, it’s like I have this internal struggle between Candis the mother and Candis the neonatal nurse. When I’m lying on that exam table, I’m not just waiting to hear the heartbeat; I’m counting the seconds, waiting for that familiar rhythmic sound, terrified of the silence. I’ve seen the things that go wrong in the NICU, and I can’t just "switch off" that clinical part of my brain. It’s exhausting, really. 

My OB is wonderful, but sometimes I think I push too hard. I’ll start asking about protocols or specific data points—things most people don't even think about—and I see that flicker of "oh, she’s one of *those* patients" in their eyes. I don't want to be difficult, but how do I stop knowing what I know? There’s a loneliness in it, too. I look at my nursery, which is mostly just a spare room right now, and I wish I had someone to just share the nerves with. It’s a very quiet, heavy kind of excitement.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

To be perfectly honest, I expected the appointments to feel like a homecoming. I’ve spent my career in the clinical world, so I walked in with a very specific, almost sterile expectation—white coats, clipboard checklists, and that clinical detachment I’ve perfected myself. I thought it would be a familiar, comforting routine. I anticipated being the "patient" in the way I’ve always treated patients: clearly, efficiently, and with the assumption that the protocols would catch anything that went wrong.

Instead, the reality felt… vulnerable. When I sat in that chair for the first time, I realized that I couldn't hide behind my scrubs or my charts anymore. The environment felt surprisingly intimidating, even though I knew the layout of the clinic better than anyone. I expected to feel empowered by my knowledge, but I actually felt exposed. The information they gave me was standard—the typical pamphlets on prenatal vitamins and avoiding deli meat—but it felt so simplistic compared to the "what-ifs" running through my head. 

I think I expected to be treated as a colleague, but they treated me like a novice. It’s hard to have someone explain fetal development to you when you’ve spent years monitoring it in high-stress situations. It was a jarring transition, realizing that no amount of clinical training can truly prepare you for the emotional weight of being the one carrying the life inside.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and it was definitely a negative one—was just how lonely the silence felt in that office. 

In my work, there is a certain rhythm to how we handle expectant mothers. It’s always been about moving through the checklist to ensure the best outcome. But when I’m the one on the table, and the doctor is making those quick, routine marks on my chart, I realize how much of this experience is treated as a "process" rather than a human event. I expected that because of my background, I’d be able to bridge that gap, but I found myself feeling invisible. 

There was this moment during my first ultrasound when I caught myself holding my breath, watching the monitor with a clinical, diagnostic eye. I was looking for the things that go wrong. When the technician just kept going through her standard script, oblivious to the fact that I was mentally dissecting every shadow on that screen, I felt this wave of isolation. I wanted someone to stop, look at me, and just say, "You’re doing a good job, and your baby is okay." I realized then that no one was going to hold my hand or acknowledge the weight of being a single mother at forty-two. I’m the professional in the room, even when I’m the patient, and that’s a very cold place to be.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a professional, data-driven conversation. In my line of work, we don’t sugarcoat things. I thought I’d be given the raw numbers, a clear breakdown of the probabilities, and a collaborative discussion on the next steps—almost like a case review. I anticipated that because I speak the "language" of neonatal health, my providers would respect my autonomy and give me the nuanced, detailed information I crave to calm my nerves.

The reality was much more… filtered. It felt like they were trying to shield me from the very data I was asking for. When I pressed for specifics on my screening results, I got vague reassurances—the kind you give a patient who isn’t a clinician. "Everything looks great," they’d say with a practiced smile. It felt condescending, even though I know they were just following their standard communication protocols. 

I found myself feeling frustrated because their desire to be "reassuring" actually spiked my anxiety. I didn't want a soft touch; I wanted transparency. It’s hard to feel like you’re being managed rather than informed. I realized that even though I’m a nurse, in their eyes, I’m just another "geriatric pregnancy" patient who needs to be kept calm. It’s a paradox: the more I try to show I know what's happening, the more they treat me like I don't.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I expected—or perhaps I just desperately hoped—for a partnership. I wanted that "gold standard" continuity where I wasn't just a chart number in the system, but a person with a specific story. I imagined a provider who would sit down, look me in the eye, and acknowledge that being a single, older first-time mom comes with a very different set of anxieties than a twenty-five-year-old in a stable marriage.

The reality has been more like a revolving door. I see whoever is on the rotation that day. Each time, I have to re-explain my background, my concerns, and the fact that I’m a nurse, which just leads to that awkward, "Oh, you work in the NICU? That must be so hard," conversation before they dive into their standardized script. There is zero continuity of care. I feel like I’m constantly starting from square one. 

Because I don't have a partner to help me advocate or just to hold the space, this lack of consistency is exhausting. I’m doing all the emotional labor of managing my own care while trying to navigate a system that seems built for efficiency, not connection. It feels less like being cared for and more like being processed. I just want to stop being a "case" and finally feel like a patient who is actually being heard.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Practically, it’s a nightmare of redundancy. Every time I walk in, I have to give a condensed version of my medical history, and inevitably, the provider stops the physical exam to ask about my career. They get so curious about my background in the NICU that we lose five or ten minutes of actual clinical focus. It’s like I’m performing a social role before I can get to the medical one.

Emotionally, though, it’s much heavier. Because I’m doing this alone, there’s no one else to keep track of the nuances. If I mention a small worry to one provider, it’s usually forgotten by the time I see the next one two weeks later. I feel like I’m carrying the entire weight of my pregnancy’s narrative by myself. There’s no one who knows the "whole" me—not the doctor, not the nurses—just a series of strangers who see a few minutes of my life and then disappear. 

It makes me feel deeply isolated. I catch myself wanting to ask, "Do you remember when I was scared about that cramp last month?" but the person in front of me has no idea what I’m talking about. It reinforces this sense that my pregnancy is just a task to be completed, and I’m just a body that needs to be cleared for the next stage. It’s draining to have to keep re-establishing my trust in a new person every single time I walk through those doors.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I think I expected a balance of clinical assessment and something approaching human warmth. Because of my training, I’m used to the clinical side—measuring fundal height, checking blood pressure, palpating for position. I expected those to be handled with a certain professional efficiency. But I suppose I expected that as my body began to change—as I started to look like an actual pregnant woman instead of just a patient—the conversation would shift to include me as a person, not just a set of vitals.

The reality has felt clinical to the point of being dehumanizing. The exams are fast, almost perfunctory. I’m often lying on the paper-covered table, exposed, while the provider is looking at a computer screen, clicking boxes to ensure they’ve checked off the right criteria for my age and risk category. It feels like they’re examining the *pregnancy* rather than examining *me*. 

When they talk about my body, it’s always in terms of risk mitigation. They don't ask how I'm feeling about the changes, or how my body is handling the shift—it’s all about weight gain, blood pressure, and potential markers. I feel like a container that’s being monitored for quality control. It’s a very strange, detached way to experience the most profound physical transformation of my life. It makes me feel small, and honestly, it makes me feel very much alone.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven't had to face a formal diagnosis like pre-eclampsia yet, but honestly, every day I wake up and check my blood pressure, I feel like I'm playing a game of "anticipatory defense." Because of my age and my line of work, I’m constantly looking for the red flags. 

In the NICU, I’ve seen the aftermath of those conditions, so I have this mental map of exactly what *could* go wrong. My expectation was that if I did develop a complication, the medical system would be a collaborative partner—that we’d sit down and manage it like a team. I thought my clinical background would make me a valuable asset in those conversations.

The reality, however, is that I feel like I’m guarding a secret. I’m so afraid that if I speak up about a minor concern—a bit of swelling or a headache—the providers will immediately categorize me as "high-risk" and start treating me with that clinical distance I’ve already felt. I’m terrified that if I get sick, I’ll be pushed into a protocol-heavy, impersonal tunnel where I lose even more agency. 

I’m managing my health with the precision of a surgeon, but I’m doing it in the dark. I worry that the reality of a complication would mean even less "me" and even more "patient." I don’t want to be a pathology; I just want to be a mother.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected the system to be a well-oiled machine. As a nurse, I know how the triage process is *supposed* to work—you call, you state your symptoms, and a professional assesses the urgency. I assumed that because I work in healthcare, I’d have a sort of "fast pass" or at least a level of clear, direct communication. I thought I’d be able to get a straight answer without playing a game of phone tag.

The reality? It’s been incredibly frustrating. When I have a concern, I’m usually met with a front-desk staff member or an automated portal system, not a clinician. I have to explain myself to three different people before I ever reach a nurse, and even then, I’m often told to just "wait for the next appointment" unless I’m bleeding or in active labor. 

It makes me feel like a burden. When I finally do get through, the advice is almost always generic. It’s hard not to read between the lines, thinking, *“If you’d just let me talk to the provider for thirty seconds, I could tell you exactly why this isn't normal.”* The system isn’t built for responsiveness; it’s built for liability and efficiency. Being on this side of the phone makes me realize how many people are falling through the cracks, feeling just as unheard and anxious as I do.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Yes, more often than I care to admit. There was a stretch in my second trimester where I had some persistent, low-level abdominal discomfort. Logically, I knew it was likely round ligament pain or just the uterus stretching, but the nurse in me started cataloging all the more serious possibilities. 

I sat there for two days, staring at my phone, debating whether to call. I knew exactly what would happen: I’d be put on hold, I’d have to convince a receptionist that my concern was "valid enough" to escalate, and then I’d likely be told to "monitor it" and come in if it got worse. I didn't want to be the "anxious older primipara" who cries wolf. I have this internal fear that if I call too often, they’ll put a note in my file—*“difficult patient”*—and the next time I have a real emergency, they won’t take me seriously. 

So, I waited. I tried to manage it myself with rest and hydration, essentially nursing myself. It’s a dangerous place to be, using my own clinical knowledge as a barrier to seeking help. I didn’t want to face that feeling of being an inconvenience or being judged for my anxiety. It’s humiliating, honestly, to be a healthcare provider and feel like you’re too much of a bother to be heard by your own care team.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I suppose I envisioned a village. When you’re forty-two and doing this alone, you cling to the idea that the people you love will step in to fill that void. I expected my friends to be excited, to drop by, maybe help me paint the nursery or just sit on the couch with me while I’m exhausted. I thought there would be a community around me, buffering the isolation.

The reality has been much quieter. People are happy for me, of course, but it’s a distant kind of happiness. They don’t know how to talk about a high-risk, solo, mid-life pregnancy, so they often don’t talk about it at all. It’s like there’s this unspoken boundary; they don't want to overstep or ask the "wrong" questions. I find myself shielding them from my true fears because I don’t want to be the "downer" or the "scary pregnant lady" who keeps bringing up NICU statistics.

It’s ironic—I’ve spent my life caring for others, and now that I’m the one who needs the support, I don't know how to ask for it without sounding needy. My community is there, but they’re standing on the other side of a glass wall. They’re cheering, but they aren’t in the trenches with me. It just adds to that heavy, lonely silence I feel in my home.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I expected—or maybe I just hoped for—a sort of "all-hands-on-deck" response from my circle. I imagined regular check-ins, someone coming over to help me build the crib, or friends offering to drive me to appointments so I wouldn't have to navigate the sterile parking garage alone. I think I wanted them to be a buffer against the medical system, to be the ones to ask, "How are you doing, really?"

That didn't really materialize. Instead, most people seem to be keeping a polite, respectful distance. I think they’re intimidated by my clinical background; they assume I "know everything" and therefore don't need help. It’s a very lonely kind of pedestal to be put on.

The support that *did* show up was a surprise, though. It’s come from these tiny, quiet places. A neighbor I’m not even that close with dropped off a bag of groceries on my porch last week with a simple note saying, "I know you're busy, take care of yourself." It wasn't a conversation about the pregnancy or the risks, just an acknowledgement that I’m a human being who needs to eat. It was so simple, yet it felt more meaningful than a lot of the well-meaning but empty "congratulations" I get from others. It’s not the village I imagined, but I’m learning that even a small, quiet presence is better than an empty house.

You know, what really strikes me now, as I sit here looking at the half-finished nursery, is the irony of my entire career. I’ve spent my life preparing for "the worst." I’ve been the one providing the expert, measured, sterilized care to hundreds of families. I thought I knew how this worked. But now that I’m the one lying on the paper-covered table, I realize that I’ve spent my whole professional life missing the point.

I’m so focused on the *outcomes*—the Apgar scores, the vitals, the potential complications—that I’ve forgotten how to just *be* a mother-to-be. The medical system is so obsessed with managing my risk that they’ve effectively erased my experience of the pregnancy itself. Every time I get a test result, it’s just another piece of data, not a moment to marvel at this life I’m creating. 

I’m tired of being the "nurse patient." I’m tired of the clinicians talking *at* me instead of *to* me, and I’m tired of my own internal monologue constantly ticking through differential diagnoses. I just want permission to be scared, to be excited, and to be uncertain without it being treated like a clinical problem to solve. I’m starting to think that the hardest part of this journey isn't the physical toll—it’s the act of reclaiming my own humanity from a system that doesn’t know how to see me as anything other than a chart. I’m really just hoping that when the baby arrives, the "nurse" in me finally retires, and I can just be Candis.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, it happens constantly, and it’s one of the most maddening parts of this entire experience. The most pervasive example is the unsolicited advice about my "age-related" risks. Every time a new provider walks into the room, there’s this immediate, patronizing push to discuss prenatal testing or specific screenings as if I’m an uninformed first-time mother. I’ve read the literature; I’ve seen the clinical data more times than I care to count. When I try to say, "I’m comfortable with this path, I’ve reviewed the risk-benefit analysis," they immediately default to, "Well, as an older patient, we really recommend..." 

It’s like my clinical degree becomes a liability. They treat my informed refusal as a stubbornness they need to overcome rather than a calculated decision. 

And then there's the unsolicited input from family. Because I’m a "NICU nurse," they assume I’m basically a medical encyclopedia. People constantly feel the need to offer their "expert" opinions on everything from my diet to how I should handle my delivery plan, as if I haven't spent my life watching the complications they’re worried about. They don't realize that their advice—meant to be helpful—just sounds like a reminder of everything I’m terrified might go wrong. It’s isolating. I’m drowning in other people’s opinions while the one thing I actually need—a moment of quiet, genuine support—gets pushed to the sidelines. I just want to stop being a textbook case and start being a mother.

You know, the hardest part is that I’ve spent my entire life being the one who "knows." In the NICU, I’m the authority. I’m the one the parents look to for reassurance, the one who explains the monitors, the one who keeps things calm. I built my whole identity on being that anchor. Now that I’m the one floating in the storm, I don’t know how to ask for help without feeling like I’m failing.

I catch myself performing. When I go to my appointments, I make sure my hair is done, I make sure I ask one "smart" question so they know I’m a nurse, and I keep my face completely neutral. I’m literally suppressing my own maternal instincts because I’m afraid that if I show any softness, or if I admit how terrified I am of that "clinical silence," they’ll judge me as incompetent. It’s like I have to prove I’m worthy of having this baby at forty-two.

It’s exhausting, truly. I want someone to look at me and see a woman who is tired, who is scared of the midnight feeding, who is grieving the lack of a partner, and who is just… in love with this tiny, flickering heartbeat. Not a neonatal nurse. Not a "geriatric" pregnancy. Just Candis. I hope that when I finally hold this little one, I can stop being the professional and just be the person. I’m so ready to stop analyzing and start living.

The silence in my home is different lately. It’s no longer just the absence of a partner; it’s a space where I’m holding my breath, waiting for the reality to catch up with the theory. 

People think that because I’ve spent my career in the NICU, I must be immune to the anxiety, or worse, that I should have "all the answers." I’ve had colleagues look at my chart and say, "Oh, you’re the perfect patient, you know what to do." And they’re so wrong. Knowing the pathophysiology of a complication—knowing exactly how a system can fail—doesn’t make it easier to sleep at night. It makes it harder. I’m not just a mother-to-be; I’m a mother-to-be who has seen the worst-case scenario written in the stars, and now I’m living through a process that assumes everything will be textbook.

I find myself walking past the nursery door, wanting to go in, but stopping. I’m afraid that if I start decorating, if I start putting up the mobiles or folding the onesies, I’m "jinxing" the clinical reality. I’m paralyzed by the need to stay detached so that if the worst happens, I’m already prepared for the NICU, not the nursery.

I want to believe that my body—this forty-two-year-old vessel—is enough. I want to let go of the clipboard and just trust that I don't need to monitor every heartbeat to prove I’m a good mother. I’m so exhausted from being the provider, the patient, and the lone guardian of this pregnancy. I’m ready to just be human again. I just don’t know who I am when I take the nurse’s coat off for good.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, absolutely. It’s like once you’re pregnant, your body becomes public property for commentary. Being forty-two, the scrutiny feels even sharper. There’s this constant, implied judgment that because I’m older, my body is inherently "less capable" of handling the pregnancy, so every pound gained or lost is interrogated as if it’s a failure of my medical discipline.

I remember one appointment, a provider—who I’d never met before, mind you—spent five minutes lecturing me on my BMI and "optimal weight gain" for my age. They didn't ask how I was eating, or if I was struggling with nausea or fatigue; they just looked at the chart, saw the number, and started the script. It felt like being back in the NICU, critiquing a patient’s failure to thrive, except the patient was me. 

It landed like a blow to the gut. I’m already hyper-aware of every physical change—I’m a nurse, I know the risks of gestational diabetes or hypertensive disorders inside and out. I don't need a lecture; I need a conversation. When they focus on the "data" of my weight, they’re ignoring the human exhaustion of growing a person on my own. It makes me feel like a machine that isn't running to spec. It’s isolating, and frankly, it just makes me want to withdraw even more. It’s supposed to be a time of celebration, but instead, it feels like I’m being graded on a performance I never asked to be part of.

I think the most frustrating part of that weight conversation—and others like it—is the assumption that I don’t already know the clinical implications. They give me these canned, cautionary speeches as if I haven’t seen the real-world outcomes of those conditions in my own practice. It’s patronizing, and it makes me feel like my clinical expertise is a burden I’m carrying, rather than an asset. 

It’s like they’re waiting for me to fail. Or worse, like they’re waiting for my "geriatric" body to prove them right. 

I’m tired of being reduced to a set of statistics. When they check my weight or my BP, they aren't looking at me, Candis, the woman who is terrified of the quiet, the woman who is grieving the life she thought she’d have. They’re looking at a patient who is "high-risk" by default. 

It makes me want to shrink away. It makes me want to stop coming to the appointments altogether because the act of going there reinforces the idea that I’m fragile, that I’m broken, that I’m "that" patient. I’m just trying to keep my head above water, and every piece of unsolicited advice, every lecture on my BMI, just pushes me a little deeper under. I’m starting to wonder if I’ll ever feel like a mother, or if I’ll always just feel like a medical case in progress.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I’ve found myself leaning heavily on a small group of former colleagues—women I worked with in the NICU who have since stepped back from the bedside. It’s a bittersweet kind of support. They’re the only ones who truly *get* the shorthand. When I’m spiraling over a symptom, I don’t have to translate my anxiety into "patient-friendly" language for them. I can say, "I’m worried about fetal movement," and they don’t give me the polite, scripted reassurances I get from my OB. They give me the honest, clinical, and often bracing truth.

But even then, it’s complicated. Sometimes I find myself scrolling through online forums or subreddits for "geriatric" or "single" mothers, just searching for a version of my own story. I want to find someone who says, "I was forty-two, I was terrified, and it was okay." I’m looking for a mirror to my own experience, but all I find are threads about the same medical risks that keep me up at night. 

Ultimately, I end up turning to my own journals. I’ve started writing down my feelings, specifically trying to separate the "Nurse Candis" observations from the "Mother Candis" ones. It’s the only place where I feel safe being truly vulnerable. No one is judging my BMI, no one is checking my blood pressure, and I don't have to defend my choices. It’s the only space I have where I’m not a patient or a provider; I’m just me, trying to navigate this massive, scary, beautiful shift. It’s a lonely lifeline, but it’s the only one that feels entirely mine.

You know, reflecting on all of this, I realize I’ve been trying to treat my pregnancy like a patient case study because that’s the only way I know how to process life. I’ve spent my career thinking that if you have enough information, if you follow every safety guideline, if you’re perfectly vigilant, you can prevent tragedy. That’s what we do in the NICU—we fight for the outcome.

But you can’t "manage" a pregnancy into perfection. You can’t control the biological uncertainty with a checklist, no matter how many years you’ve spent in the field. And yet, the system I’m currently navigating seems built on the lie that you *can*. They want my weight, my blood pressure, my age, my history—they want to turn me into a set of inputs so they can predict an output. It’s a mechanism for anxiety, really.

I’m struggling to let go of the idea that I need to be the "good, compliant patient" to ensure my baby is safe. I’m starting to wonder if the best thing I could do for this little one isn't to look at another clinical pamphlet, but to actually give myself permission to be a person who is currently terrified and completely overwhelmed. I keep waiting for the "maternal instinct" to override the "nurse instinct," but maybe that’s not how it happens. Maybe it’s not a switch; maybe it’s just the slow, painful process of letting go of the control I thought I had. I just hope I can find my way to that place before the delivery room doors close behind me. I’m so tired of being the person in the white coat, even when I’m the one wearing the hospital gown.

I think about the delivery room constantly. As a nurse, I’ve walked into that room a thousand times with that specific, cool-headed focus required to handle a crisis. I know the sounds—the alarms, the hushed medical shorthand, the frantic movement of the team. I’ve been that team. And now, I’m picturing myself in that bed, and the fear isn't just about the medical risks; it’s about the loss of identity.

If something goes wrong—if that "clinical silence" I’ve seen so many times actually happens to me—I don’t know if I can be the patient who just follows orders. I don’t know if I can be the "good, quiet mother" who trusts the process blindly. I’m going to be that woman who’s probably trying to diagnose her own labor, analyzing the fetal heart rate monitor, and questioning every intervention. 

I’m terrified that I’ll be labeled "difficult" during the most vulnerable moment of my life because I couldn't stop being a nurse. And yet, I don’t know how to stop. It’s who I am. It’s what kept me going through my divorce, through the heartbreak, through the long, quiet nights of realizing I’d be doing this solo. 

I hope that when the time comes, someone in that room recognizes that my questions aren't a sign of mistrust—they’re a sign of a woman who just wants to be a mother. I hope they see the person behind the credentials. I really, truly hope I’m not alone in that room, even if I’m physically the only one there. I just want someone to hold my hand and tell me it’s okay to stop knowing everything. It’s time I finally let someone else take the clipboard.

It’s funny, I’ve been thinking a lot about the word "patient." It’s supposed to describe someone undergoing medical treatment, but lately, I’ve been dwelling on the other definition—being patient. I’ve never been good at that. I’m a creature of efficiency, of quick clinical assessments and immediate triage. But this pregnancy… it’s the exact opposite. It’s slow, it’s agonizingly gradual, and it’s completely outside of my control.

I look at the other women in the waiting room sometimes—the ones who seem so blissfully, or perhaps just naturally, unaware of the statistics. I envy that. I don't mean that in a malicious way; I truly envy that they can sit there and just be excited about the ultrasound. They aren't counting the seconds of the heartbeat to check for bradycardia; they’re just listening to their baby. I want that. I crave that.

But I feel like I’m stuck in this purgatory between the professional I’ve been for twenty years and the mother I’m desperately trying to become. I’m starting to wonder if the "nurse" part of me is a suit of armor I’ve worn for so long that I’ve forgotten how to take it off. If I take it off, what’s left? Just a forty-two-year-old, divorced, single mother, scared out of her mind. That feels like a very dangerous, very fragile person to be. But maybe that’s exactly who needs to be in that delivery room—not a nurse, just a mother. 

I’m trying to practice it. Tomorrow, I have another check-up. I’ve promised myself I’m not going to look at the screen as a diagnostic tool. I’m going to look at it as a window. It’s a small goal, but it’s the only one I have right now. Maybe I can leave the clipboard at home, just for a little while.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every single one of them. It’s not just about having someone to hold my hand or drive me home—it’s about the fact that there is no one else who is "in this" with me at the same level of intensity. When I go to a scan, I’m the only one who sees that image and understands the weight of what’s on the screen. My friends are supportive in the abstract, but they don't know the specifics of my pregnancy, and the providers? They’re just doing their jobs.

There’s a specific kind of hollowness when I’m sitting on that table, the gel cold against my skin, and the tech is narrating what she sees. I’m thinking about the future, about how this little one will be raised, and I’m looking at the empty chair next to me. It’s a physical reminder of the divorce, of the life I thought I’d have, and of the fact that I am the sole author of this child’s safety. 

If there’s a decision to be made, or a moment of pure, overwhelming fear, I don’t have a partner to process it with. I have to be both the decision-maker and the one who absorbs the stress. I find myself wanting to turn to someone and say, "Did you see that movement? Isn't it incredible?" but the only person I have to talk to is the one who is checking my boxes. I feel like a ghost in my own life sometimes—observing my own pregnancy from a distance, because I don't have that mirror of a partner to ground me in the reality of it. It’s just me and my clinical brain, locked in a room together, waiting for the unknown.

You know, there’s a certain weight to the silence after I leave the clinic and get into my car. It’s that moment where I realize I’ve successfully "performed" the role of the capable, informed professional for another hour. But then the adrenaline fades, and the reality of the empty passenger seat hits me. I’m driving home to an empty house, to a nursery that’s mostly a collection of boxes, and there’s no one to call and say, "The baby was moving so much today, they looked like a little gymnast." 

I find myself talking to the baby, of course. I tell them everything. I tell them about the anatomy scan, about the measurements, about how the doctor said I was doing "great." It’s my way of trying to build that connection, to make this feel less like a clinical process and more like a human beginning. But it feels one-sided, and that makes me ache. 

I’m starting to wonder if this isolation is the price I’m paying for waiting this long, for doing this on my own terms. I wanted to be established, to be secure, to be someone who could provide everything—and I am. But I’m realizing that “everything” doesn’t include the shared, quiet wonder of creating a life. I’m learning how to hold that wonder all by myself, and it’s heavy. It’s the heaviest thing I’ve ever carried. I just hope that when they’re finally here, the silence doesn’t feel quite so loud.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

I do it almost every single day, and I hate it. I feel like a frantic medical student—or worse, a hypochondriac—but I can’t stop myself.

When I’m in the office, the information I get is so curated. It’s all "everything looks good, everything is within normal range." They’re protecting their liability, but they’re also silencing my curiosity. I’m looking for the *why* and the *what-if*. I want to see the specific studies on placental perfusion in forty-two-year-old women, or the latest data on early labor indicators that aren't just "go to the ER if you’re bleeding." I’m looking for the raw, technical data that I know how to interpret, but that they refuse to discuss with me because I’m "the patient."

It’s insulting, really. I’m a neonatal nurse; I have years of experience with sick babies. I know how to handle the truth, and I know how to process a complication. When they withhold information, it doesn’t calm me down—it makes me assume they’re hiding something from me. 

So, I turn to journals and clinical databases online. I’m looking for the details they skip: the nuance, the risks that aren't on the pamphlet, the reality of the statistics. I’m trying to bridge the gap between their "everything is fine" and my internal knowledge of everything that could potentially be falling apart. It’s a vicious cycle of trying to outsmart the anxiety, and it’s exhausting. I just want someone to sit down with me and treat me like a peer, to talk to me about the real, messy science of my own pregnancy. Is that really too much to ask?

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that keeps echoing in my mind is the word "permission." 

I spent twenty years as a professional being the one who grants permission—permission to be worried, permission to ask for more tests, permission to have a voice in a clinical setting. Now that I’m the one lying on the exam table, I realize how much the system relies on the patient waiting for that permission. I’ve felt like I had to earn the right to ask, the right to be heard, and the right to be seen as more than a high-risk statistic.

I want maternity care to be a space where a woman’s own knowledge—whether she’s a nurse or a school teacher or an accountant—is treated as a partnership, not an obstacle to be managed. When you strip a woman of her agency, you’re not just managing a medical event; you’re eroding her transition into motherhood. I’m starting to believe that the "clinical silence" I’m so afraid of isn't just about the monitor; it’s the silence that happens when the system stops listening to the human being in front of it.

I just want to walk into that delivery room as a mother, not a colleague, and feel like I’m safe enough to let go of the control. Thank you for listening. It felt… honestly, it felt like the first time I’ve been able to breathe through this in a long, long time.
